cross_connections.display_label: First cousin; Navy commander at Mobile Bay and New Orleans

narrative_blocks.header: Captured Fort Fisher with two thousand prisoners
narrative_blocks.body: In January 1865 he commanded the second expedition against Fort Fisher after Butler's failure, capturing the Confederate stronghold along with over 2,000 prisoners and 167 cannon. He was promoted to Brigadier General in the regular army for the victory.

narrative_blocks.header: Self-taught general with no military training
narrative_blocks.body: He was a New Haven court clerk and Yale Law graduate when the Civil War began, with no prior military experience. He has been called one of the most capable generals to emerge from the war without previous training.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent Custer up the Rosebud at Little Bighorn
narrative_blocks.body: As overall commander of the 1876 Centennial Campaign, he wrote orders sending Custer up the Rosebud on June 22. His column arrived after the massacre, sparking decades of dispute over whether Custer had disobeyed his orders.

narrative_blocks.header: Last military governor of Atlanta opposed the Klan
narrative_blocks.body: In 1868 he negotiated the Treaty of Fort Laramie that ended Red Cloud's War. He then served as the last military governor of the Third Military District at Atlanta in 1869, where he was a strong opponent of the Ku Klux Klan.

narrative_blocks.header: His line ended at New Haven, no children
narrative_blocks.body: He died at New Haven in December 1890 at age 63, having never had children. His siblings included Harriet Wadsworth Terry and Jeremiah Wadsworth Terry, names their father chose for the Wadsworth-Hooker ancestry.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law before turning to military career as Union general.

notable.notable_blurb: 4th Mayor Hartford; US Congressman; bank president

burial.plot_notes: d. New Haven CT. Burial location unverified — possibly Grove Street Cemetery New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of Hartford for seven turbulent years
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the 4th Mayor of Hartford from 1824 to 1831, spanning the city's transition from Federalist to Whig politics. He had previously served one term in the US House of Representatives (1817-1819) as a Federalist, and was a delegate to the 1818 Connecticut Constitutional Convention.

narrative_blocks.header: President of Hartford Fire Insurance for 25 years
narrative_blocks.body: He served as president of the Hartford Fire Insurance Company from 1810 to 1835 — a 25-year tenure spanning the company's early growth into one of New England's leading insurers. He simultaneously served as president of the Hartford Bank from 1819 to 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of an author and a Union general
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Wadsworth married Nathaniel Terry, one of Hartford's most prominent citizens, in 1798. The Hooker-Talcott blood that ran through her passed to her grandchildren: Rose Terry Cooke became one of New England's most celebrated regional authors, and Gen. Alfred Howe Terry led the Union assault on Fort Wagner and later commanded the Department of the Dakotas — present at the aftermath of the Little Bighorn. Catherine never lived to see either of them distinguish themselves; she died in 1841.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
